content.text: Why Changelog Generation Differs for Claude Repos

Standard software projects generate changelogs that list features, fixes, and dependency bumps. Claude application repos carry an additional obligation: any consumer of the service — whether that is an API client, a downstream team, or an on-call engineer reviewing an incident — needs to know specifically what changed in the AI layer, not just that 'version 2.4.0 was released'.

A changelog entry that reads 'updated prompt' is as useful as a changelog entry that reads 'changed code'. The AI-specific commit types defined in the previous sub-subtopic exist precisely so that automated tooling can generate changelogs that separate prompt changes from tool schema changes from evaluation suite updates — the three layers that matter most for reasoning about AI behaviour across releases.

This sub-subtopic defines how to configure semantic-release for a Claude repo, how the commit type vocabulary maps to changelog sections and version bumps, how to write a custom plugin that attaches eval references to changelog entries, how to publish the resulting release notes to GitHub, and how to write unit tests that verify the changelog tooling behaves correctly when the commit history changes.
The Automated Changelog Pipeline

attrs.alt: Automated changelog pipeline: commits → commit-analyzer → version bump → CHANGELOG.md → GitHub Release

content.text: The pipeline has four stages:
commit-analyzer
: reads the commit history since the last release tag and computes the appropriate semantic version bump. Breaking changes (!) → MAJOR; prompt/tool/agent/feat → MINOR; fix/eval → PATCH.
release-notes-generator
: groups commits by type and produces the human-readable changelog body, with custom section names for the AI-layer types.
changelog plugin
: writes the generated notes into CHANGELOG.md and commits the updated file.
GitHub release
: creates a git tag (v2.4.0) and a GitHub Release page with the release notes attached.
Configuring semantic-release
Install the required packages:
The release configuration lives in .releaserc.json at the repo root:
Running the Release in CI

# .github/workflows/release.yml
name: Release

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

permissions:
  contents: write       # create release and push changelog commit
  issues: write         # close issues referenced in commits
  pull-requests: write  # update PRs referenced in commits

jobs:
  release:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0        # semantic-release needs the full tag history
          persist-credentials: false

      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: 20

      - run: npm ci

      - name: Run semantic-release
        env:
          GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
        run: npx semantic-release

The release job runs only on pushes to main, so every merge from a feature branch potentially triggers a release. When no AI-layer commits are present (only chore or ci commits), semantic-release exits with no release and no tag is created.
Sample Generated Changelog
With the configuration above, merging a typical sprint's worth of work produces a changelog structured around the AI layers:

# Changelog

## [2.4.0](https://github.com/example/claude-app/compare/v2.3.0...v2.4.0) (2025-06-14)

### ⚠ Breaking Changes

* **triage-agent:** switch response format from prose to structured JSON. Callers
  must deserialise the content field as JSON. See migration guide in
  evals/triage/format-migration.md. ([8d36f48](…)) Eval-ref:
  evals/triage/refusal-v4.json

### Prompt Changes

* **triage-agent:** tighten refusal boundary for off-topic legal questions
  ([8d36f48](…))
* **summariser:** add length-control instruction for long-document inputs
  ([c72a1f9](…))

### Tool Schema Changes

* **tool-schema:** add optional `source_url` field to citation tool ([a91b2e3](…))

### Agent Logic Changes

* **routing:** add intent-based routing v2 with confidence thresholding ([f40d1c2](…))

### Evaluation Suite

* **eval-suite:** add 40 adversarial refusal cases for triage agent ([e15a9b7](…))

### Bug Fixes

* **triage-agent:** prevent empty-string intent when no tool call is returned
  ([d90b3e2](…))

---

## [2.3.2](https://github.com/example/claude-app/compare/v2.3.1...v2.3.2) (2025-05-30)

### Bug Fixes

* **summariser:** handle None return from claude-haiku-4-5-20251001 when
  context window exceeded ([b22f4a1](…))
Attaching Eval References to Changelog Entries

By default, semantic-release generates changelog entries from the commit subject line only. For Claude repos, reviewers also want to see the eval that certified each AI-layer change. A custom plugin extracts the Eval-ref footer and appends it to the entry:
Register the plugin in .releaserc.json after the release-notes-generator:
Custom Changelog Script for Simpler Setups

Teams that do not need full semantic-release can generate a changelog directly from git log using a Python script. This approach is easier to customise and does not require a Node.js release pipeline:

# scripts/generate_changelog.py
"""
Generate a Markdown changelog from the git commit history between two
release tags. Groups entries by AI-layer type with custom section names.

Usage:
    python scripts/generate_changelog.py v2.3.0 v2.4.0

Output is printed to stdout so the caller can redirect it to CHANGELOG.md
or append it to an existing file.
"""
import subprocess, re, sys
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from collections import defaultdict

SECTION_NAMES = {
    "prompt": "Prompt Changes",
    "tool":   "Tool Schema Changes",
    "agent":  "Agent Logic Changes",
    "eval":   "Evaluation Suite",
    "feat":   "New Features",
    "fix":    "Bug Fixes",
}
HIDDEN_TYPES = {"chore", "docs", "ci"}

# Sections in this order, breaking changes first.
SECTION_ORDER = ["prompt", "tool", "agent", "feat", "eval", "fix"]

@dataclass
class CommitEntry:
    sha: str
    subject: str
    ctype: str
    scope: str
    breaking: bool
    eval_ref: str | None
    prompt_hash: str | None
    description: str  # subject without type prefix

def get_commits(from_tag: str, to_tag: str) -> list[CommitEntry]:
    """
    Return a list of CommitEntry objects for every commit between
    from_tag and to_tag (inclusive of to_tag, exclusive of from_tag).
    """
    fmt = "%H%x00%s%x00%B"
    result = subprocess.run(
        ["git", "log", f"--pretty={fmt}", f"{from_tag}..{to_tag}"],
        capture_output=True, text=True, check=True,
    )
    entries = []
    for block in result.stdout.strip().split("\n\n"):
        lines = block.splitlines()
        if not lines:
            continue
        # Each block starts with: sha\x00subject
        parts = lines[0].split("\x00")
        if len(parts) < 2:
            continue
        sha, subject = parts[0], parts[1]
        body = "\n".join(lines[1:]) if len(lines) > 1 else ""

        m = re.match(r"^([a-z]+)(?:\(([^)]+)\))?(!)?: (.+)$", subject)
        if not m:
            continue
        ctype, scope, bang, desc = m.group(1), m.group(2) or "", m.group(3), m.group(4)

        if ctype in HIDDEN_TYPES:
            continue

        eval_ref_m = re.search(r"^Eval-ref:\s*(.+)$", body, re.MULTILINE)
        hash_m = re.search(r"^Prompt-hash:\s*(.+)$", body, re.MULTILINE)

        entries.append(CommitEntry(
            sha=sha[:8],
            subject=subject,
            ctype=ctype,
            scope=scope,
            breaking=(bang == "!"),
            eval_ref=eval_ref_m.group(1).strip() if eval_ref_m else None,
            prompt_hash=hash_m.group(1).strip() if hash_m else None,
            description=desc,
        ))
    return entries

def format_entry(e: CommitEntry) -> str:
    """Format one CommitEntry as a Markdown list item."""
    scope_prefix = f"**{e.scope}:** " if e.scope else ""
    line = f"* {scope_prefix}{e.description} ({e.sha})"
    annotations = []
    if e.eval_ref:
        annotations.append(f"eval: `{e.eval_ref}`")
    if e.prompt_hash:
        annotations.append(f"prompt-hash: `{e.prompt_hash}`")
    if annotations:
        line += " — " + ", ".join(annotations)
    return line

def generate_changelog(from_tag: str, to_tag: str) -> str:
    """
    Build the full Markdown changelog string for the range from_tag..to_tag.
    Breaking changes are listed first; all other entries follow grouped by type.
    """
    commits = get_commits(from_tag, to_tag)
    sections: dict[str, list[str]] = defaultdict(list)
    breaking: list[str] = []

    for e in commits:
        formatted = format_entry(e)
        if e.breaking:
            breaking.append(formatted)
        sections[e.ctype].append(formatted)

    lines = [f"## [{to_tag}] — {from_tag}..{to_tag}", ""]

    if breaking:
        lines.append("### ⚠ Breaking Changes")
        lines.extend(breaking)
        lines.append("")

    for ctype in SECTION_ORDER:
        entries = sections.get(ctype, [])
        if not entries:
            continue
        section_name = SECTION_NAMES.get(ctype, ctype.title())
        lines.append(f"### {section_name}")
        lines.extend(entries)
        lines.append("")

    return "\n".join(lines)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    from_tag, to_tag = sys.argv[1], sys.argv[2]
    print(generate_changelog(from_tag, to_tag))
Testing the Changelog Tooling
Unit tests for the custom changelog script ensure that the section grouping, breaking change detection, and eval-ref annotation behave correctly when the commit history changes:

# tests/test_generate_changelog.py
"""Unit tests for the custom changelog generator."""
import unittest
from unittest.mock import patch
from scripts.generate_changelog import (
    CommitEntry, format_entry, generate_changelog,
)

def make_entry(**kwargs) -> CommitEntry:
    """Create a CommitEntry with sensible defaults."""
    defaults = {
        "sha": "abc12345",
        "subject": "prompt(triage-agent): tighten refusal boundary",
        "ctype": "prompt",
        "scope": "triage-agent",
        "breaking": False,
        "eval_ref": None,
        "prompt_hash": None,
        "description": "tighten refusal boundary",
    }
    defaults.update(kwargs)
    return CommitEntry(**defaults)

class TestFormatEntry(unittest.TestCase):

    def test_basic_entry(self):
        e = make_entry()
        result = format_entry(e)
        self.assertEqual(result, "* **triage-agent:** tighten refusal boundary (abc12345)")

    def test_entry_with_eval_ref(self):
        e = make_entry(eval_ref="evals/triage/refusal-v4.json")
        result = format_entry(e)
        self.assertIn("eval: `evals/triage/refusal-v4.json`", result)

    def test_entry_with_prompt_hash(self):
        e = make_entry(prompt_hash="a3f2d9c1e7b40512")
        result = format_entry(e)
        self.assertIn("prompt-hash: `a3f2d9c1e7b40512`", result)

    def test_entry_no_scope(self):
        e = make_entry(scope="", description="global safety update")
        result = format_entry(e)
        self.assertNotIn("**", result)
        self.assertIn("global safety update", result)

class TestGenerateChangelog(unittest.TestCase):

    def _mock_commits(self, entries):
        return patch("scripts.generate_changelog.get_commits", return_value=entries)

    def test_breaking_changes_appear_first(self):
        entries = [
            make_entry(ctype="prompt", description="normal prompt change"),
            make_entry(ctype="prompt", breaking=True, description="breaking format change"),
        ]
        with self._mock_commits(entries):
            changelog = generate_changelog("v2.3.0", "v2.4.0")
        breaking_pos = changelog.index("⚠ Breaking Changes")
        prompt_pos = changelog.index("### Prompt Changes")
        self.assertLess(breaking_pos, prompt_pos)

    def test_hidden_types_excluded(self):
        entries = [
            make_entry(ctype="chore", description="upgrade deps"),
            make_entry(ctype="prompt", description="update refusal boundary"),
        ]
        with self._mock_commits(entries):
            changelog = generate_changelog("v2.3.0", "v2.4.0")
        self.assertNotIn("upgrade deps", changelog)
        self.assertIn("update refusal boundary", changelog)

    def test_section_names_are_ai_specific(self):
        entries = [
            make_entry(ctype="prompt", description="prompt change"),
            make_entry(ctype="tool", description="tool schema change"),
            make_entry(ctype="agent", description="agent logic change"),
            make_entry(ctype="eval", description="eval suite update"),
        ]
        with self._mock_commits(entries):
            changelog = generate_changelog("v2.3.0", "v2.4.0")
        self.assertIn("### Prompt Changes", changelog)
        self.assertIn("### Tool Schema Changes", changelog)
        self.assertIn("### Agent Logic Changes", changelog)
        self.assertIn("### Evaluation Suite", changelog)

    def test_empty_commit_list_produces_header_only(self):
        with self._mock_commits([]):
            changelog = generate_changelog("v2.3.0", "v2.4.0")
        self.assertIn("v2.3.0..v2.4.0", changelog)
        self.assertNotIn("###", changelog)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    unittest.main()
Keeping the Changelog Useful Over Time
Changelog quality degrades when team habits change. A few operational rules prevent this:
Problem
Symptom
Fix
Commit subject too vague
'prompt: update' appears in changelog — no context
Add a commitlint rule: subject-min-length: [2, 'always', 20]
Missing scope
Multiple unrelated changes appear under 'Prompt Changes' with no scope prefix
Enforce scope-enum in commitlint — see previous sub-subtopic
Breaking change without body
Changelog shows '⚠ Breaking Changes' but no migration note
CI check: any commit with ! must have a BREAKING CHANGE: footer
Eval-ref points to deleted file
Changelog annotation links to a file that no longer exists
validate_eval_refs.py in CI catches this on the PR
Chore commits accumulating in changelog
CHANGELOG.md fills with noise
Ensure chore is in the hidden list in releaseRules

The most important rule: the changelog entry is written by the commit message, and the commit message is written when the change is made. Teams that defer changelog writing to release day always produce shallow, inaccurate changelogs. The conventional commit convention makes this automatic — the changelog is a byproduct of writing good commit messages.
Summary

Automated changelog generation for Claude repos extends the standard semantic-release workflow with AI-layer-specific section names and version bump rules. The commit types defined in the previous sub-subtopic (prompt, tool, agent, eval) map directly to changelog sections that make it easy for operators, integration owners, and product teams to scan the release notes for the changes that matter to them. A custom plugin attaches Eval-ref and Prompt-hash footers to changelog entries, preserving the traceability chain from commit to deployed behaviour. Unit tests on the changelog tooling ensure that the section grouping and annotation logic remain correct as the commit convention evolves.
